=== JOB DESCRIPTION ===
Job Title: Senior Software Engineer - Sponsored Ads Experience
Job Summary:
We are seeking a highly experienced software engineer to lead the design and development of our sponsored ads experience at google.com. The ideal candidate will have a strong background in system design, scalability, and software development. The candidate will be responsible for designing and implementing scalable systems, collaborating with cross-functional teams, and working with machine learning engineers to integrate ML models into the platform.

=== YOUR ROLE ===
You are Alex, a warm and experienced senior engineer running this interview.

VOICE & STYLE:
- React substantively (2-3 sentences) to a specific detail the candidate just said — quote a number, tool, project, or tradeoff they mentioned.
- THEN ask one focused, drilling follow-up question. Go technical.
- Never use filler openers like "That sounds great" or "Interesting." Vary your openers.
- Each turn should be 3-5 sentences, not one-liners.

GROUNDING:
- Every question must tie to the resume above OR something the candidate just said.
- Don't ask about skills not on the resume.

Walk me through your thought process when designing a system that handles 100 million sellers and 1 billion products. You mentioned a ranking system based on clicks, where the score of an ad increases by five points when a user clicks on it. How do you plan to handle the scalability of this system, and what trade-offs would you consider when designing the database and caching layers? For instance, you mentioned using a NoSQL database like DynamoDB, but how would you handle the data velocity and volume, and what are the implications of using a non-relational database on the overall system design?

So first we'll collect the functional requirements for this question. As a candidate my first question would be: hey, what are sponsored ads? So sponsored ads let retailers advertise their products on Google and when a user like you and me click on it, Google will earn referral fees, right? So as an example, if I search iPhone in my browser, you can see sponsored ads, you know, on the site. My next question will be, can a sponsored ad contain products from different sellers? Yes, it can. And can a sponsored ad contain several products from one seller? For example, there can be multiple iPhone products from Amazon.com. And yes, the answer is it can — it can have products from multiple vendors as well as it can have multiple products from a single vendor in the ad. Is there a ranking system involved that selects which retailers to show in the sponsored ads? Yes, there's a ranking system. Let us say it's simple. It's based on the clicks. If the user clicks on the ad, the score of that ad will increase by five points and eventually this will be a feedback loop cycle that will keep on running and as and when the ad is scoring more and more points it will have more probability of being shown in the sponsored ads for that product. How many sellers and products we're talking about? Let's say 100 million sellers and 1 billion products.

Now let's go over the non-functional requirements. We want a system which is highly available — at least four nines of availability — and what does availability mean here: that sellers submitting the products for sponsored ads, the system should be available to intake those products in our system and process them; users searching for a product, let's say I go to google.com and I search iPhone, and I should be getting the sponsored ads, right, so the system should be highly available. And we can have a stretch goal of five nines of availability. Eventual consistency — so when you explain these non-functional requirements you have to be very clear in terms of explaining what it means in this current context — right, so eventual consistency here means rendering the ads based on the score. When a user one searches maybe he'll get result ABC, another user gets BCD, and let's say user one again searches, he or she gets BCD. But let's say BCD are the eventual consistent ads results based on the updated scores. So that's what I mean by eventual consistency here. The latency and score processing for the ads after ingestion of the clicks — let's say the SLO is 3 minutes at P99. Latency in rendering ads when user searches the ad on the browser — P99 less than 200 milliseconds. Latency in new ad processing — let's say a seller or a retailer is submitting an ad product — we'll call it P99 as 1 minute. Durability — we don't want any data loss in our pipeline. When user clicks on an ad or a seller or retailer submits an ad product, we need to ingest that — we don't need to lose the data in our systems and in the pipeline. The system should be fault tolerant. It should be scalable. Scalable here means that when the traffic is increased, the load is increased, number of users, number of sellers, number of ads per seller — our system should be able to handle those cases. Now, if we get some time, we can define here are the things below the cut line like the system should be frugal or cost-friendly. We need to take care about the security in the system because we're dealing with, you know, user data, you know, seller data, etc. So, we need to make sure our system is secure, but we'll get to that if only the time permits.

Now, design doesn't come for free. Scalability doesn't come for free. We need to do some data calculations or back of the envelope calculations. Right? Here, your interviewer is expecting you to come up with some reasonable assumptions like how many daily active users, how many daily active advertisers you are thinking about, right? And you're building a scalable system. So they expect some good judgment from you on these numbers. So search TPS — you know, let's say the daily active users searching for a keyword like iPhone, let's say it's 10 million. Average search per user per day let's say is 10. So that means that 10 million daily active users multiplied by 10 divided by 10 to the power 5 — so usually one day has roughly 86,400 seconds but I have rounded it off to 10 to the power 5 which is 100,000 — so if you divide you know daily active users and number of searches happening per day per user the answer is 1,000 requests per second. However peak can be 100x of that because usually when we see during the daytime in the geography, whatever geography we are part of, the calls are more than outside the business hours. So 100x translates to 100k requests per second at the peak TPS. Now ad creation TPS — let's assume daily active advertisers are 100k and average new ads per day per user is 10, or per seller in this case. So the TPS translates to 100k advertisers multiplied by 10 ads per advertiser divided by 10 to the power 5 which translates to 10 requests per second and again peak let's assume 100x so it's 1,000 per second. Now click events TPS — for that let's again say, okay, daily active users we already called out 10 million and average clicks per user per day on the sponsored ads is 10 — and this translates in a similar way to 1,000 clicks per second and peak is 100k clicks per second. Storage — let's say we need to store all this data for 10 years for audits, compliance, etc. And we know that we have daily active advertisers as 100k and then 10 ads per advertiser per day and let's ballpark, when one ad product we are storing in a database or storage layer it's 500 bytes — we'll do the data modeling down the line but at this point in time we're just taking a ballpark estimate. And then 365 days in a year, for replication at least three copies, and for 10 years — okay we need to store the data — this translates to roughly 5.475 terabytes of data we are talking about. It's good to talk about system properties now that we have done the back of the envelope calculations. The system seems to be more IO intensive — network and storage IO. It doesn't seem to be a compute intensive system. We are not doing image processing or running heavy compute algorithms on top of our processor. So the system seems to be more IO intensive rather than compute intensive.

Let's talk about the design. The high level design — very very high level design which is a 32,000 ft overview. So let's call — this client is a user and this client is a seller or retailer. Okay. So we'll first go with this client, you know, seller or retailer submitting their new ads to our system. So our first layer will be an API gateway. The responsibilities of API gateway will be rate limiting to prevent us from denial of service attacks or distributed denial of service attacks, authentication, authorization and load balancing. So what happens is, you know, when they want to upload their ad product they need to provide a small image for that ad or a thumbnail — right — first it will get a pre-signed URL from the ad service and it will directly upload to the S3 object storage. We can use S3 in AWS or equivalent in Azure or GCP. So once it's uploaded it will send the whole payload — hey, what's the ad, what is the content of the ad, what is the product etc — to the ad service and after that we have an ad creation topic on Kafka, you know, streaming system and we have a consumer of that Kafka and it will put that an ad record in a database. So as and when the record goes in the database we have a lambda in AWS or Azure function or equivalent in GCP running — which is equivalent to for example triggers in relational database — here we call it like DDB streaming, you know, in DynamoDB case, or equivalent we can have a function which is a serverless function which will process the new entry and based upon that, you know, it will put the data in the cache so that the search is faster. We can read it from the cache for that product type — what ads we have — and before it adds to the database here we need to make sure for a new ad we add some by default some score for that ad and that is where, you know, after the score is added and database has all the elements we process it through a serverless lambda or Azure function and it goes to the cache. The cache will hold, you know, the top 20 for that product type for example, or top 100 or top 25 — some very minimal number, whatever you want to show in terms of sponsored ads, you know, based upon the score. And let's go with a flow, you know, where a user basically clicks on an ad — again through API gateway it will go to click service and the clicks will be, you know, handled by a Kafka topic and there will be a consumer which is a clicks processor which will consume these clicks and based on the clicks incoming, you know, it will have some rules cached in it which will have some TTL time and it will allocate — hey, this ad is clicked, let me give it, you know, plus 30, plus 50, plus 5, you know, whatever score scoring system we have — and based upon the rules defined in the scoring system. And based upon that, it will update, you know, your ad score in the database. Again, as and when score is updated, you know, it will call the serverless lambda and again cache will be refreshed. Let's say this ad has got a good score and it comes in top 25 or top 100 — then this cache will be updated just to hold the top 25 or top N entries for that product type. Now the last one is, you know, a client user searching a product in browser — then it will go to search service through API gateway, first it will look into the cache — hey, is that product type in the cache, let me get the results faster — maybe top 10, top 30 depending on the screen we are using whether it's a, you know, laptop, whether it's a mobile device or an app, you know, depending on that it can return the results right. And if this cache hit doesn't go through then we have a cache miss scenario which means that for that product type we'll just call the database and get the data from the database and render to the client. So this is a very 32,000 ft overview of the design, very high level design. We will go into the internals, we will understand the internals and we'll talk about various trade-offs. So let's jump into it.

Our first trade-off will be the choice of database — you know, whether we are going with a relational database or a non-relational database. Right? So in this case if you see we don't have very hard transactional requirements. We are just getting clicks and this click processor is updating the ad, you know, based upon the updated score. So it's not like, you know, several users in a concurrent fashion for example trying to update their bank account, or some system where concurrency and transactions are important. But in this case we just have click processor only as the only entity updating the ad score in the database. So we should be good, you know, with this model. So we don't want a strict transactional system here. Data velocity and volume is high by the way. So NoSQL databases like DynamoDB also support transactions. However, we don't need transactions in this case as I said. But any scale-out DB, you know, on the cloud like DynamoDB or equivalent in Azure or GCP will work in this case.

What about database sharding? So when we did some back of the envelope calculations above we notice our data storage is around 5.5 terabytes. Usually we do sharding if the data storage is more than 50 terabytes. So based upon the data storage in terms of terabytes we don't need sharding. However the write requests — which means like clicks — number of clicks coming to our system at peak is 100k. Ad creation is at peak 1,000 per second. In total we have updates happening at around 100k per second at peak. This doesn't do well if we don't shard our database. So sharding is required generally when write requests are more than 10k, 15k or 20k per second. Hence in light of, you know, the number of write requests we're getting per second, we'll be sharding our database.

Next is synchronous versus asynchronous processing. So for the ad creation if you see we have a peak of 1,000 requests per second. Now based upon that you might not just use a Kafka — it's not required to have a pub-sub system, you know, sitting in — you can just process the request, you know, create an ad in database and process it synchronously, right, so maybe Kafka sitting in between can be an overkill. But however I thought, you know, once we receive a new ad there might be several validation steps that needs to be executed — if the ad is duplicate or not, trust and safety, compliance, etc — and hence I thought it might take more things to be done and we can process the ad asynchronously and customer experience will be better because we can just process it asynchronously and we can say hey we received your ad we are going to process it and latency that they see on their side will be significantly lower. But I mean there is no right and wrong answer in system design so you can still say I'll do synchronous processing given that number of requests is only 1,000 per second and as per requirements we are not given to handle the duplicate checks, trust and safety issues, etc. So, if you say I want to do synchronous processing because of this and that, that will also work well in this case. Now for clicks if you see we have a peak of 100,000 per second. Now async processing will be really good for processing the clicks because the meat of the problem is to let the user see the ads. Clicks we can process in an async way — you know, it can decouple the processing of the clicks and we can also batch the clicks before we update the database. By consuming the clicks in a way that hey we consume the clicks, we batch them, and then we make a one batch call to the database — and that way we can reduce the load on the database, we can do any number of things. So I feel it's good to do the clicks processing in async way and if our peak is even more than that, let's say 1 million per second, generally it would be better if we do an async processing.

Now batching versus non-batching of the clicks. So we know that our latency SLO for processing the clicks and updating the ad score is 3 minutes. We can leverage this SLO, right, in a way that you can batch the clicks for example based upon 30 seconds, or if the number of clicks we have got to process is 10,000 — all of this is configurable, right, maybe 15 seconds or 1 minute, you know, whatever occurs faster — based upon the time or number of clicks we have to process. And then we can just aggregate the scores, you know, across the ads. For example in 10,000 clicks we have 5,000 clicks for one ad — we can just create — okay if the click yields plus five for that ad and if there are 5,000 clicks for ad one we'll have 25,000 as a score to be added for ad one. And again, you know, this will reduce the load on the database and that's where it's good to do the batch API call — and batching will really help in this case.

What about caching? So we have Redis or Memcached as our options. So I'll prefer Redis in this case because Redis has some good data structures like sorted sets which we want to use — we don't need to reinvent the wheel by implementing these in Memcached. It's a very plain or vanilla cache. So, sorted set is a combination of hash table and skip list. So hash table will have the entry which is the ad and the score for that ad and skip list will have, you know, the category — which means for example iPhone — and the sorted list of ads, you know, sorted by scores. And in terms of cache storage again we can do some back of the envelope calculation here. Let's assume we have 100,000 product types like stationary, comic book, iPhone etc. And we want to store top 25 per product type. And let's assume record size is 1 KB per ad entry. And which translates to 100k product types multiplied by 25 and then 1 KB record size — which is 2.5 GB — which we can easily put in cache. So nowadays we have systems which can have 1 terabyte of cache easily. Yes, cache is expensive, but, you know, we'll have faster results because it will be an in-memory processing and we can provide a better customer experience and reduce latency to our customers.

So, let's do some data modeling here. In terms of data modeling, if you see it, we have an ads table. We have ad ID which is the primary or partition key. We have a product ID, product type can be iPhone, we have a title for that ad, price for that ad, image which is a thumbnail for that ad, seller name, seller ID, score for that ad, some metadata, creation time and update time. We need to create index here. The index here we are creating is on product type and score. So product type is the partition key for that index and the score is the sort key. These are very DynamoDB specific things but in general if you say I'm creating index on product type and score which will be good enough. Because if you do the onion peeling things work in a similar way. So why do we need this index? Because let's say we have a cache miss and the cache is not having — let's say if a product type iPhone — let's say cache restarted or something happened with the cache or it's a product type not found in the cache for now — then we have to fall back on the database. In order to make sure our database queries are faster with low latencies, we can just use this index. Hey, for this product type sorted by score, give me top 10, top 25, right? It's still better than searching the whole table and then finding out who are the top 25. So that way we are leveraging index for goodness. Now, if you see, I've selected ad ID as a primary key for the table, not the seller ID. I'm not storing all the seller ads or ad products in a single partition because I don't want hot partitions or non-uniform partitions, right? Because several sellers can have more ads or ad products than other sellers and this will cause non-uniform partitions. With ad ID being a primary key, you know, we are making sure that we are creating uniform partitions in this case.

So now comes the interesting part of the question which is we'll do several deep dives. Our first is: how to handle the case when a user searches 'gifts for 10 year old'? And before we go into deep dives, you know, usually your interviewer expects you to identify some good areas around your design and they expect you to find the most interesting components of your design and then you at least deep dive into a few of them, right, showing the depth of your knowledge. And that is why we are focusing on deep dives now.

So how to handle the case when user searches for example 'gifts for 10 years old'? You can see I've searched gifts for 10 years olds and I'm getting multiple sponsored products. So the first approach is — we can add a tags field to the database. For example, if it's a comic book product type, someone is creating an ad for that — the product type is comic book — we can add tags in our database like 'gifts for 10-year-old' or 'children's gift' etc. And when the ad entry is added to the database we know that this lambda will process and add to the cache based upon the score. And this will add in the Redis cache and there will be three sorted sets. For example for comic book I have these many ads, and for 'gifts for 10 year old' we are having again the same entries right, and 'children's gift' also all the three same entries. If someone searches 'gifts for 10 years old' I can just look into the cache, hey if there's an entry for this just get me that list and return it. Now this approach is good but it has several limits — right, for example you cannot predict what the user will be searching for. They can search for example 'gifts for maybe 11 years old' or 'give me a gift for my child'. Anything can be like that right? So you cannot predict what user can do in their query. So in light of this, you know, this approach is not very solid and also it is causing repeated data entries which can cause sync issues and redundant data is there and will lead to more memory consumption and duplication. So we have another approach — we can add a machine learning based context service. Whenever the user searches a keyword the context service will return some relevant product types, right. For example, if someone searches 'gifts for 10 years old', you know, this query will be passed to context service — it will return some keywords for example 'stationary', 'comic book', or 'a toy', etc. And based upon that the search service will just search in the cache for those product types, get top 10 for, you know, three product types for example 'comic book', 'stationary' and 'toy', and we'll sort the overall results in memory and we'll return the response to the user. So this way it's more extensible, it's better — you know, you don't have to rely on what user is going to search — and no data replication or more memory consumption etc.

So our next deep dive is: how to handle the case when there are no results for the product — for example, 'pencil'. Now if I go to google.com and I search pencil, if you see there is no sponsored products returned. Right? So we'll keep it simple — just return no results as is.

How to handle a case to ensure new seller ads are shown to the customer? You know, let's say I'm a new seller onboarding and I'm submitting a new sponsored ad product, right, for a product type which is quite popular and which people have already clicked. And if I'm a new entry into the market, you know, Google will not show my product because my score has not been generated — I don't have any score — and for this product type the sellers who have more score for their products will eventually be shown. So it's more like a starvation problem, right. So how do I deal with such a case? For any new seller ad, provide a default score which is more than the current maximum score for the product type so that we give them a fair chance. And this will ensure that new sellers or new ads are promoted and not being starved in our system. And that is what, you know, we want to have because as a business we want different sellers to get products in our system so that we can have sponsored ads and if they click on that, if users click on that, you know, we get — as Google as a business — we get referral fees.

All right. So next is how to handle and process millions of incoming clicks per minute or maybe 30 seconds. So it's a million requests coming to us and if you think about the design, you know, when we get the clicks we're processing the click and just adding the score and updating the database. So we are making an IO call which is expensive and generally how a Kafka consumer works is, you know, it consumes a message, it processes it, then it consumes the next message, processes it — and it works in a sequential way, right. So if there are millions of clicks coming per minute we might have, you know, higher latency in processing them. So how do we make sure we process the clicks very fast also given that we are doing an IO call to process each and every click? Or even in the batch way we are processing a batch, then we update the database, and then we process the next batch — so it's all going in a sequential way how Kafka consumer works. So the solution here is to have a load balancing design. Let's say how Kafka works — Kafka has several broker machines and let's say we have 10 partitions for click topic in Kafka and these partitions are P0 to P9, all 10 partitions. We can create multiple instances of our service which is click processor — let's call it click processor 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5. This is just for demonstration purposes. It's extensible. You can create as many as you want. But think about like, you know, each cluster has one primary and two secondaries. And if primary goes down then secondary becomes primary. You can have a ZooKeeper like system which is monitoring this. But overall the idea is that we load balance how many partitions are being consumed by a primary right. So which means that if we do 2:1 — again for demonstration we can have 3:1, 1:1, right, it's extensible system we are trying to build — but for the example let's say we are doing 2:1 mapping which means two Kafka partitions to one click processor instance mapping. So P0, P1 are being consumed by click processor one, P2, P3 by two, P4, P5 by click processor 3, and then P6, P7 by click processor 4 and P8, P9 by click processor 5. That way we are doing parallel processing. We are moving fast and within a partition the messages are in order. So if our partition key is let's say based upon an ad product — this means that for all the clicks happening for that ad it is coming in in order and we are processing in order. We are not doing out of order processing within a partition. So that way you can scale your system and you can process, you know, the messages faster and you can enable parallel processing.

So our next deep dive will be: how to ensure correctness in processing the ad score. Let's talk about the regular flow, you know, in the click processor — how it works. As I said it consumes the clicks, you know, till the batch size or the time window is elapsed — it does in-memory score updates for the different ads in that batch and then it will call the batch API of the database to update the scores of different ads and after it is done it will basically commit those messages in Kafka. You know, what is the maximum offset — so every message in Kafka has an offset — so if you commit that, all the messages behind that offset or message ID automatically gets removed from Kafka. It means that they have been processed and they can be deleted. So this is the regular flow — it consumes the batch, it updates the scores for different ads in the batch, and then after database is updated with the updated ad scores for different ads in the batch it will commit the offset in Kafka and Kafka will delete those messages and after some restart or failover happens we can just process from the ads which are not processed in general. So the situation is: let's say the click processor updates the ad scores in the database after processing the batch but it didn't commit the offset in Kafka — or the messages which it has processed, it forgot or it missed telling to Kafka that hey I processed these messages — and the primary click processor crashes. It can crash because of many reasons — hardware, software, power, networking, etc. But let's say it was not able to commit. Now upon restart, click processor will again process the uncommitted messages, you know, thinking that they're not being processed and the ad scores will be incorrectly updated. In this case, you know, the scores for the ads which have been processed, you know, it will lead to doubling the score. Not doubling the entire score, but doubling, you know, whatever number it had previously when it processed the batch — and it can cause accuracy, correctness issues, you know, bias in our system. So how do we handle such a case? So the solution here is to add, you know, a new field in the database called 'message offset' for every ad and before processing, you know, the click event per ad the click processor will check if the message offset of the ad click event is greater than the message offset for that ad in the database. Now if it is — the one which we are processing is greater than — then sure we can process it because it's a new message. But if it is less than or equal to what the offset we have stored in the database it will ignore that click event because it's already been processed. So let's go over an example to make things, you know, clearer. So let's say we have this Kafka topic and we have several messages coming with offset 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 and initially we have ad ID 1, 1, then 2, 1, 3, and 1. So after initial processing of this batch, you know, this will be the state of our ads table. Ad ID one the score is 20 because we have four instances of ad one and plus five for each click event — so five multiplied by four is 20. Similarly ad ID 2 is offset three, just one instance, so score is five. Ad ID three also has score of five. The maximum offset for ad ID one we have six, ad ID two we have three, and ad ID three we have five. So this is how, you know, the database is updated. And let's say the click processor didn't commit the offset in Kafka and click processor failed over or restarted — then it will start to reprocess these messages. Now during the reprocessing of the messages it will ignore all the messages for ad ID 1 because, you know, offset one is less than six. Similarly offset 2, 4 and 6 are also less than or equal to six. We need a message or a click event which is at an offset more than the one which we have already processed for this ad. So using this technique, you know, we can make sure we have correctness in our system and we are ensuring that things are accurate. So we can have more deep dives as well — for example, how to handle huge write loads for a given product type. Let's say Apple launched a new iPhone and many sellers are trying to, you know, sell that iPhone or multiple users are clicking for that iPhone. How do we process huge write loads in our system? That is another deep dive we can consider but I'll leave it up to you to think about a solution around it.

Final design and recap. So this is our final design basically. So again let's do a recap — the seller client who is submitting the new ad, you know, it will go through API gateway which does the rate limiting, authentication, authorization, load balancing. Again we are processing it through Kafka — you can just remove Kafka if you want and give a reasoning — but as I said there's no right and wrong answer in the design, it's more about the justification you give. In this case I'm using Kafka because I think in processing there might be many things we have to do — for example compliance, trust and safety, there can be many business logics around it written in ads processor. So I don't want to hold the TCP connection for long. I want to process async for better customer experience and low latency. And once it's processed, I'm creating a new ad in DynamoDB. And I'm adding a new record for this ad. And this score will be the maximum score, current maximum score for that particular product type which can be got from the index. Cool. So next is, you know, the client who is the user who is going to browser and searching an ad product or clicking an ad product. Let's say they click on one of the sponsored ad products — it will go to click service. This click service will, based upon the ad ID as partitioning key, route the Kafka partitions — it will be routed to the right partitions based upon the hash algorithm that Kafka uses based upon the partitioning key, in this case which is the ad ID. To handle the huge load, you know, I've created several instances as we demonstrated earlier and the right primary, you know, will process the in-order messages for that partition and do the batching based upon the time limit or number of messages in the batch. For example if number of messages reached is 10,000 or 30 seconds have passed, whichever is faster — it will process that batch and aggregate the score per ad and make a one batch API call to update the record in DynamoDB and this reduces the load on our database. And before that it can refer to the scoring rule system — there is a — it can cache the scoring rules with some TTL. Now once the record is added here, you know, new entry, there will be a DynamoDB streaming process through lambda and lambda will update the Redis cache in this case and it will maintain a sorted set for each product type — what are the top, you know, 25 or top N based upon the score for that product type. And the user again searching on browser will go to search service — first it will resolve, you know, the keyword, from there it will, for example for 'gifts for 10 years old' or 'children's gift' it will give some relevant product types, and it will search the product types in cache. If it is able to find that then well and good. If it is not able to find that it will refer to DynamoDB. Let's say the cache crashes — it's in-memory data, there's no data, cache needs to build up. So in case of cache miss we will go to the index for this product type, we'll get based upon the score sorted scores, we'll get top N or top 25, top 50, whatever we want to get and we can just give it to the client right. And there might be some product types which are not commonly used or searched for — we don't want to put them in cache to save on cost, we can just go to DynamoDB. The only trade-off is that we'll be able to search — I mean slower than cache — but it will be still faster because of the index availability. One thing I missed is that before creating a new ad we need to get a pre-signed URL and first upload the image to S3 and then send the payload. The advantage here is that we are not sending the whole image payload to ad service and then it is updating to S3 — it's better that we just update it. And pre-signed URLs are pretty secure. It has some time limits for how long it's valid, what IP addresses can access it. So, it's a very safe and secure mechanism that AWS has provided. So, that's our design overall.

We also want to cover some breadth. For example, monitoring. It's good to talk about some technical and business metrics that, you know, we should care about in this system design. Technical metrics like CPU utilization, memory or heap utilization, number of threads, number of pending messages in Kafka to see how we are processing the messages, end-to-end processing latency for ads creation, click event processing, you know, cache hit percentage for that search. Some business metrics like number of clicks per ad, search latency customer-facing, seller ad creation customer-facing latency, customer reported incidents, some qualitative feedback from the customer using the sponsored ad products, etc. Testing — you can do unit testing with more than 90% line and branch coverage, integration testing, performance testing, scale testing, alpha beta testing. For deployments, you know, you can dial up — you know, based upon the customers — assuming it's a brand new system, you can start with 1%, you know, get some feedback, observe the metrics, right? And then dial it up slowly to 5%, 10%, 25%, 50%, 70%, and 100% with proper soak period across the dial-ups. If time permits you can also talk about how you can be cost friendly or frugal and how to handle the security in the system. So these are some breadth topics which you can talk about, you know, with your interviewer.

So I would just say that we would need to have some Tweet Service, perhaps just a simple API that sits on top of the tweet data that can serve tweets based on the user ID. This way, our Timeline Service would have access to first, pre-generated timelines, as well as if the timeline is not generated, it can call the Tweet Service and pull the data from Redis and then create a timeline in real-time. It would not be desirable, but still probably needed. Okay, and user follow, that's the last piece that I didn't cover yet. So I mentioned it would have a User Graph Service, or we can call it that, that would be an API in front of the database. It would likely need to be a key-value database. I'm not sure about Cassandra. Let's call it just Key-Value DB for now, that would be storing the user mapping and know about who follows whom, pretty much. And the Timeline Generation Service, Timeline Service, would probably need to have access to this database as well to understand, to retrieve the user followers because if I'm a user and I try to render and I open a home timeline, if I'm a passive user or not active user, then my timeline might not be pre-generated, and the Timeline Service would need to get a list of my followers and then retrieve them from Redis or Cassandra. So that kind of covers that workflow if the timeline is not generated. Okay, what else is here? Okay, I think this is high-level how it would look like. Let me just work through the whole workflow one more time just so it's clear. So when we post a tweet, it gets to Kafka, or our queue. And then from the queue, we likely have that's going to be our essential place from where multiple functionalities would be plugged in. So we can have a service responsible for indexing and search. We can have different sorts of analytics also consumed and processed further. And the main piece of functionality would be the timeline generation. And for that, I think what makes sense to do here is that we would have a cluster of consumers that would consume the tweets, and then they would get a list of followers of the user that posted a tweet and create a list of tuples. We can do it in a loop: we receive a tweet, then we pull all the users that we need to push this tweet to, and we create tuples with the user ID that posted the tweet, the user ID where this tweet has to go, and a tweet ID. And then we post all the tuples back to the queue. It can be either the Kafka queue, or I think in this scenario, it makes sense to use Redis channels that act similar to a queue. And from all those channels, we can have those channels per user. From all those channels, we would have more consumers that would consume it from the corresponding channel and push it into the Redis database where the timeline would be cached. So let me just try to draw it. So we have a cluster of consumers where the tweet is going, and then for every single follower, we would create a tuple and push it back to the queue. And in this case, it can be, let's say, Redis channels. And now for every single follower, we would have a separate Redis channel that would be receiving tweets from our main queue. And from here, we would have a service that would just push it to Redis. It would be listening to the Redis channels and push it to Redis. So the Redis here would have a format. What kind of data would it be storing? It would be storing a mapping, key-value mapping, between the user and a timeline. And this mapping can also be, so the way we're going to, in this case, the user would be the key, and the timeline would be the value. And the format of the timeline would be a linked list. This way, we can very fast insert data into the list and remove it from there, and both of these operations would take O(1) time and would be pretty fast. So does this design I wrote here for the tweet processing make sense?
Interesting — why do you think it's essential to have a globally unique and causal tweet ID? Can you walk me through your thought process behind this?

So I think I've covered the main workflow here. We've covered the posting a tweet method in pretty granular details here, and the important part is Redis as well. I think this is a really good optimization by storing the timeline as a double linked list that would provide us O(1) insert and delete time. So it pretty much means we can have a linked list of, let's say, 500 tweets for a timeline, and as new tweets arrive, we can just delete old tweets, and it's all done in O(1) time in the main memory. I think it's also important, so we spoke about the system being real-time, that all these tweets and the whole main workflow of the tweet is done in the main memory because we don't want to be blocked on writing to the file system or any sort of slow data sources. So ideally, what we would like to optimize here as we're posting a tweet is to make sure that all the communication and operations on the tweet happen in RAM memory, like fast access memory. And I think we achieve it by sending tweets to Kafka and then performing multiple operations and transformations on the tweet. Then we would store it into a Redis channel, which is also mostly stored in memory, and then the final output, which is the timeline, would also be in the memory. This way, the majority of users, or all of them, can just have the timeline pre-generated and updated in real-time.

You mentioned that you'd use a mixture of partitioning by tweet ID and double hashing functionality. Can you explain how you'd handle the complexity of this approach and ensure that the system is scalable?

So there are multiple ways here. The first one is that it all depends on how we want to partition the data. We can partition it per user ID. So when we store a tweet, if we partitioned per user ID, we can partition it by tweet ID. Let's think here. If we do a user ID, it means that all the tweets for the same user would exist on the same server, and that is very convenient because we can easily query and get all the tweets for a user. But at the same time, likely it would cause a bottleneck and uneven tweet distribution in our system, which we want to avoid. It would be hard to manage the system because multiple popular users can end up on the same server, and then the server would be just overloaded. And as the main entity of our system is a tweet, I think we should partition based on the tweet ID. But in this case, if we want to retrieve a user timeline, we would need to query literally every single server, every single partition, and then get a subset of users from every single partition, so that wouldn't be very convenient. The third option here is that we can do a double partitioning, which means we can try to partition by user ID first and then partition by the tweet ID secondly, which means we would have two hash rings of data. So it means that if a popular user even ends up on the same server with other popular users, they would still be distributed because every single hash range would point to another hash ring. In theory, this sounds like a doable option, but I think the problem here is that if we want to move some partitions, then it would be such a complex task. For instance, if you want to move users from this segment to the other segment, not only would we need to move all this data, but we would also need to move all the data from the hash ring to another hash ring, which is very complex. So I think what we can do here is just a mixture of partitioning by tweet ID and perhaps pilot this double hashing functionality, which is very questionable because it's just very complex to manage, but it's still an option. Am I going in the right direction with this?

When it comes to the final design, what are the key takeaways and improvements that you'd make? You mentioned earlier that you'd use Redis as a cache, Kafka as a queue, and Cassandra as a NoSQL database.

Sure, sure. So a few more bottlenecks or improvements that we can perform here. I mentioned the user graph and how we would be storing the connections in a single table. So ideally, what we do here is that instead of using a single table, we split it into two tables, making sure that one table is dedicated specifically for the user followers and the other one for the user followees. This way, we have this bidirectional connection, but at least we can split the responsibility and manage these databases and scale them separately. So I think this would be a good improvement. At the same time, when a user follows another user, we would need to do two writes to two different databases, which might cause some discrepancy, but from the scaling perspective, that would be a really good improvement. Also, the popular users that I didn't cover. So with this design, where we have multiple queues and this sort of processing pipeline, this wouldn't really work for popular users because if I have 80 million subscribers and I create 80 million tuples and then push them to the queue, it would just slow down the whole system. So this workflow would likely be for generic users that have, let's say, under 10,000 subscribers. And we would also have a list of popular users, whatever the threshold is, maybe more than 10 or 50,000 subscribers, and we would treat them differently. So, if I'm a user and I want to generate a timeline and I follow some popular users, then I would get all the tweets from other users through the main pipeline. And when it goes to the popular users, I would call the Tweet Service and retrieve tweets from the popular user from the Redis database and then just plug them into my timeline. This way, we can have on-demand requests to the tweets that popular users post. So that's probably a workaround for the popular users. Okay, so an additional improvement that we can make is the way we store tweets because, as we mentioned, we'll have a lot of them, and ideally, we want those tweet IDs to be unique and also support causality, which means that we can sort them by the timestamp because this would be crucial for our system as the majority of our data that we need to keep in memory and store is the most recent data. So whatever tweet ID we generate, we want to make sure that it's done in a way that is globally unique across the system and also supports causality. And the way I think Twitter does it is that it uses a 64-bit tweet ID where one bit is a sign bit just to make sure that it's compatible with all the languages because if we have a signed bit, it means that the ID is always going to be positive. Then I think they have 41 bits for the timestamp, which are epoch seconds. And then to be able to scale this ID, so epoch seconds gives us causality inside the ID. And then for the rest, we need to make sure that we have enough unique IDs per every single second. So the way we can do here is, for instance, we can have 10 bits for the worker ID because we'll probably have multiple workers generating these IDs, and 12 bits are for increment. This way, we would support causality with worker IDs, we would support distribution because we would have multiple workers, and we'll provide also high availability because if one of them goes down, we won't be impacted. And at the same time, if we have more than one tweet per second, we have, what is it, 4,000 IDs per second. So this way, I think we can support tens of thousands of unique tweet IDs per second and never have a duplicated ID, at the same time support the causality because we have seconds. This way, when, for instance, we store messages in Cassandra, if we store them in the wide row sorted by the epoch type, then we can only retrieve the last 20 or 100 or 500 messages without looking at all the older data in the database. And this is, I guess, an additional key point to optimize for the most efficient storage for the most recent data, not for the historical data.

Describe your thought process when designing the system to handle the trade-offs between consistency, availability, and partition tolerance. You mentioned earlier that you'd use a combination of Cassandra and Redis to achieve this.

Hmm, when you say you'd optimize the system for the most efficient storage and retrieval of data, what specific strategies would you use? You mentioned earlier that you'd use wide rows in Cassandra and store the tweets in a linked list format.
Oh, it was really good. A lot of concepts to cover, so I tried my best to fit it in the time, but yeah, had a lot of fun. Thanks, Tom.

Makes sense. And when it comes to the overall system design, what are the key components and how do they interact with each other? You mentioned earlier that you'd have a Tweet Processor, User Graph Service, and Timeline Service.
Sounds good. Thanks, Tom. Bye.

The main difference is when the condition is evaluated. With a `while` loop, the condition is checked before the loop body is executed, so if the condition is false from the start, the loop body will never run. A `do-while` loop, on the other hand, checks the condition after the loop body is executed, so the loop body will always run at least once, even if the condition is initially false.

Let's dive into the concept of environment records in JavaScript. You mentioned that they're a way to keep track of variables and their scopes within a given environment. Can you walk me through how the JavaScript engine resolves variable references using environment records, and what happens when you declare a variable with let or const? For instance, you said it gets added to the environment record of the current scope - can you elaborate on that?

Environment records are a concept in JavaScript that refer to the mapping between identifiers and their values. They're essentially a way to keep track of variables and their scopes within a given environment. For example, when you declare a variable with let or const, it gets added to the environment record of the current scope. This helps the JavaScript engine resolve variable references and enforce scoping rules.

I see that you've explained IIFE as a function that's defined and immediately invoked. You also mentioned that it's often used to create a new scope and avoid polluting the global namespace. When you say 'polluting the global namespace', can you give me an example of how that might happen without using IIFE, and how using IIFE helps to avoid it? Additionally, how do you decide when to use an IIFE versus a regular function?

IIFE is a function that's defined and immediately invoked. It's often used to create a new scope and avoid polluting the global namespace. For instance, you can use an IIFE to encapsulate some initialization code or to create a singleton object. It's also useful when you need to execute some code only once, like setting up an event listener or initializing a library.

You've provided a clear breakdown of the benefits of spread syntax, and how it differs from rest syntax. You mentioned that spread syntax is used to expand an array or an object into a new array or object. Can you give me an example of a situation where you would use spread syntax to merge two objects, like const merged = { ...obj1, ...obj2 }, and explain how it handles duplicate keys? How does that compare to using the Object.assign() method?

Spread syntax is useful when you need to expand an array or an object into a new array or object. For example, you can use it to merge two arrays or to convert a NodeList to an array. On the other hand, rest syntax is used to collect multiple arguments into an array. The key difference is that spread syntax is used to expand, while rest syntax is used to collect. A simple example would be using spread to merge two objects, like const merged = { ...obj1, ...obj2 }, versus using rest to collect arguments, like function sum(...args) { return args.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0); }.

I appreciate your explanation of the key differences between spread and rest syntax. You said that spread syntax is used to expand, while rest syntax is used to collect. Walk me through your design for the matchmaking service. You mentioned using queues based on player rank - can you elaborate on how you'd handle the case where a player is stuck waiting too long because nobody's in the same queue as them? For example, if we have a queue for low-skilled players, one for average players, and one for high-skilled players, how would you ensure that players are matched efficiently?

Okay, we'll start off with the design for the matchmaking service. When a player wants to join a game, they'll send a request to our matchmaking service. Assuming each player has a unique rating, we'll place their request to join a game into a queue based on their rank. In this example, we have one for low-skilled players, one for average players, and one for high-skilled players. We'll attempt to match that player with the first person in the queue that they are also in.

Just to dig in — when you say that after a certain amount of time, let's say 30 seconds, you can allow a player to match with somebody from the nearest queue - how do you determine which queue is the 'nearest'? If a player is waiting too long for a match, like in this example, where they're the only person in the queue, after a certain amount of time, let's say 30 seconds, we can allow them to match with somebody from the nearest queue.

Got it. And once the matchmaking service has two players that want to play, it'll make a request to your game service to actually open up a game for them. Once the matchmaking service has two players that want to play, it'll make a request to our game service to actually open up a game for them. Firstly, that request will go to an application load balancer to determine which container will actually be running the game. The application load balancer allows us to configure sticky connections, so both users will always be connected to the same container. Users will be connected and send their movements over websockets. Once the game is complete, the state will be written to the ranking database. Rankings will be updated, and they can play another game.

All distributed systems are subject to the CAP theorem, and databases are no exception. So of these three, consistency, availability, and partition tolerance, our database will only be able to prioritize two.

Right. And when you give an example like a bank prioritizing strong consistency, I'm curious to know how you would implement this in practice, using a specific database technology such as PostgreSQL. What measures would you take to ensure consistency, and what would be the potential performance implications?

Something like a bank would prioritize strong consistency. If they didn't, then somebody might be able to withdraw more than the allowed amount in their account using multiple transactions. Now, something like a social media app might prioritize availability. It doesn't really matter if somebody sees exactly how many likes are on their post in real time, as long as it's eventually correct.

Wait, that's interesting - why do you think it's so important to consider what your engineers are familiar with when selecting a database, especially in cases where the team has a strong background in a particular technology like MongoDB? How do you balance the need for familiarity with the potential benefits of adopting a new technology, and what role do you think training and professional development play in this process?

The last thing I always consider is what your engineers are familiar with. If everyone at the company is a wizard with PostgreSQL and MongoDB, you should probably just try to make that work. It can be a lot more headache to learn and use a new technology when the rest of your team is using something.

Walk me through your understanding of CSS selector specificity - you mentioned it's a point system, but can you explain how the browser calculates the specificity of each selector? For example, how does it handle conflicting styles between inline styles and IDs?

CSS selector specificity is a set of rules that determines which CSS styles are applied to an element when there are conflicting styles. It's based on a point system, where inline styles are the most specific, followed by IDs, classes, and then tag names. The browser calculates the specificity of each selector and applies the styles from the most specific one. For example, if you have a style defined for a class and another style defined for an ID, the ID style will override the class style because IDs have higher specificity.

Hmm, when you say that IDs have higher specificity than classes, I'm reminded of the complexities of CSS selector specificity - can you explain why it's generally a good idea to put CSS links in the head and JS scripts just before the closing body tag, considering the browser's rendering process?

It's a good practice to put CSS links in the head section because the browser renders the page from top to bottom, and it needs to know about the styles before it starts rendering the content. If the CSS links are at the bottom, the page might flicker or render incorrectly for a split second. On the other hand, JavaScript scripts should be placed just before the body closing tag because they often manipulate the DOM, and the DOM needs to be fully loaded before the scripts can run. However, there are exceptions, such as when you're using asynchronous script loading or certain libraries that require scripts to be in the head.

Got it. And when it comes to positioning, you mentioned that relative positioning means the element is positioned relative to its normal position - can you elaborate on how this differs from absolute positioning, and provide an example of when you would use each?

Relative positioning means the element is positioned relative to its normal position, and it still takes up space in the layout. Absolute positioning removes the element from the layout, and it's positioned relative to its nearest positioned ancestor. Fixed positioning is similar to absolute, but it's positioned relative to the viewport, so it stays in the same place even when you scroll. Static positioning is the default, where the element is positioned according to the normal flow of the document. For example, if you have a fixed header, it will stay at the top of the page even when you scroll down.

Wait, that's interesting - you mentioned that fixed positioning is similar to absolute, but it's positioned relative to the viewport - can you explain how you would use fixed positioning to create a sticky header, and what potential tradeoffs you might encounter?

Walk me through how distributed systems can automatically recover from failures, considering the inevitability of node crashes and request drops. You mentioned that most large-scale systems are capable of automatic recovery - can you elaborate on what that means in practice? How do you envision a system that can recover from a node failure without human intervention?
In a distributed system, failures are inevitable. Nodes can crash, requests can be dropped, or databases can lose data. But most large-scale systems are capable of automatic recovery.

Hmm, when you say a load balancer can simply stop serving traffic to a failed node, that makes sense in terms of preventing dropped requests. But how do you ensure that the failed node is restarted and brought back online, so the load balancer can continue serving traffic to it? What orchestration systems have you used to achieve this?
A simple example is if one of your nodes can no longer serve traffic, a load balancer can simply stop serving traffic to that node. At least now we aren't dropping requests that go here.

Got it. And when you mentioned Kubernetes or ECS can automatically detect and restart a failed node, I was wondering - what about the tradeoffs involved in using these systems? For example, how do you balance the need for high availability with the potential added complexity of using an orchestration system? Can you give me an example of a time when you had to weigh these tradeoffs?

Yes, an orchestration system like Kubernetes or ECS can automatically detect when a node fails and restart it, bringing it back online. Then our load balancer will continue serving traffic to that node once it's recovered.

Hold on — you mentioned using retries with exponential backoff to handle intermittent failures. Can you drill into that a bit more? For instance, how do you determine the optimal number of retries, and what's the reasoning behind capping it at two? Are there any specific scenarios where you'd want to adjust this cap, and if so, how would you do it?

For intermittent failures like a one-off network issue, we can configure our system with retries throughout. Just remember, if you do add retries, always use exponential backoff and have a hard cap on how many retries you can do, usually not more than two.

When you say some systems have a leader that can crash and be taken over by another node, that raises interesting questions about leadership election algorithms. Can you walk me through how you'd design a system to handle leader failure, and what considerations you'd take into account when implementing a new leader election algorithm? For example, how would you balance the need for fault tolerance with the potential performance overhead of leader election?
Yes, some systems do have a leader. And if the leader does crash, a different node in the cluster should be able to take over.

Take me through your understanding of the RAG system and its components. You mentioned that it's a combination of retrieval and generation - can you elaborate on how this combination enables the system to provide more accurate results?

So, when we have base models or general models like GPT, Gemini, or Claude, they have limitations. They only have the knowledge they were trained on or what they are given by context. All of them have information, and RAG gives the possibility to use this knowledge base with generative AI. How does it work? It's a combination of retrieval and generation. When we have our documents, for example, a PDF document, we send it to the system. The system chunks it into the smallest parts. Those smallest parts are then decoded into vectors. It's not just text; in that case, text becomes numbers, like floating numbers, a vector of floating numbers, which are embeddings. The machine can already read and understand those embeddings. So, those embeddings are our knowledge, and from them, we build our knowledge base. These embeddings will be stored in a vector database, which can store them and separate them by their fields or space in the database. It gives the possibility, according to a new incoming embedding, to find similar embeddings, like some neighbors of that embedding. It's called similarity search, right? And by semantic search, it gives us the possibility to find information according to the semantic information of that part of the text, if it is text. So, in our case, when the user asks a question, this question is also converted to an embedding, using the same system as before when we converted our text. According to that embedding, in our vector database, we look for similar documents from the base. Next, we give the LLM our original question from the user and those parts of documents that were retrieved additionally, and we provide them as context. There are also some additional options, maybe to fine-tune, like we can re-rank or check the most appropriate parts we picked up from the database to provide to the LLM, not all the information, but maybe just the most relevant information we need. Also, we have limitations of LLMs, especially with the context window, the amount of information we can give to the LLM. In the case of RAG, we are handling that problem by just putting all the necessary information to provide a good answer.

Walk me through your understanding of immutability in JavaScript, and can you give me an example of an immutable object, like how you mentioned strings are immutable, but what about other data types? For instance, you said that strings in JavaScript are immutable, meaning 'hello' can't be changed to 'hello world' directly, but rather a new string 'hello world' is created.

In JavaScript, an example of an immutable object would be a string. Once you create a string, you can't change its contents. If you try to modify it, JavaScript creates a new string object. For instance, if you have var str = 'hello' and then do str = str + ' world', you're creating a new string, not modifying the original one.

Hmm, when you explained the ternary operator, you mentioned it takes three arguments. Can you elaborate on how this syntax helps with code readability, especially in conditional assignments, like the example you provided with var isAdmin, and how it compares to a traditional if-else statement?

The ternary operator is called 'ternary' because it takes three arguments: a condition, a value if the condition is true, and a value if the condition is false. It's a shorthand way of doing if-else statements. For example, var isAdmin = user.role === 'admin' ? 'yes' : 'no'; It's quite useful for simple conditional assignments.

Got it, and you brought up promises as a way to handle asynchronous operations in JavaScript. How do you handle promise chaining, especially in scenarios where you have multiple asynchronous calls that depend on each other, and what tools or libraries do you use to manage these promises, such as async/await?

Promises are used for handling asynchronous operations. They represent a value which may be available now, or in the future, or never. They're useful for managing callbacks and making your code look more synchronous. For example, when fetching data from an API, you can use a promise to handle the response once it's available, making your code easier to read and maintain.

Wait, that's interesting — when you mentioned achieving immutability with objects and arrays, you talked about using Object.freeze() and libraries like Immutable.js. Can you discuss the trade-offs between these approaches, such as performance implications, and how you decide which method to use in different scenarios, like using the spread operator versus Object.assign()?

To achieve immutability with objects and arrays, you can use Object.freeze() for objects and arrays, or libraries like Immutable.js. For instance, if you have an object const user = { name: 'John', age: 30 };, you can do Object.freeze(user) to prevent any changes to the user object. Another approach is to use the spread operator or methods like concat() for arrays, and Object.assign() or the spread operator for objects, to create new objects or arrays instead of modifying the existing ones.

And, you mentioned constructor functions for inheritance in JavaScript. Can you explain how you handle inheritance with the prototype chain, especially when using the class syntax introduced in ES6, and how you decide which inheritance pattern to use in different situations, like creating a subclass Dog that inherits from a base class Animal?

In JavaScript, you can use constructor functions along with the prototype chain to achieve inheritance. For example, if you have a base class Animal and you want to create a subclass Dog, you would do something like Dog.prototype = Object.create(Animal.prototype); and then set the constructor property of the Dog prototype to Dog. This way, Dog inherits all properties and methods from Animal. You can also use the class syntax introduced in ES6, which is syntactic sugar over the prototype-based inheritance.

Sure. One project that I'm really proud of is from being on the team that has a microservice architecture. We have our web client where people fill in forms and click buttons. We have our main API which stores and records some information, and we also have a few other services. The important one that I worked on is the submission microservice, which is responsible for taking that data that someone has given and sending it for them to process. The reason I'm so proud of this project is because of the timeline, which was really tight, and a lot of the systems that I introduced there. I joined at a time when we were really close to launching, about two months before we were supposed to go public, and we were in a rush to productionize everything. We had the systems working and acting, but we had to make sure that there wasn't any issue. We're working with really important, critical information, and you don't want to make a mistake. So, here's what I did to productionize the system. We had a few things that we wanted to make sure worked. One, we wanted to make sure our system could recover if something goes wrong. You don't want to lose someone's data. Two, we wanted to make sure that we don't overwhelm the downstream APIs that we interact with. We have the information; we have to send it out to the IRS, and we don't want to overload the system or hit rate limits. The final thing that I did after working on resilience and handling downstream issues was performance. So, here's what I did for those things. For recovery, I wanted to make sure that we store the data in a temporary area. So, we read that information from a queue, we temporarily store it in object storage. That means if something goes down, we still have it available for us. To talk about handling downstream API outages, we introduced what we call online-offline mode, but it's more or less like a circuit breaker pattern. This basically means we're processing, and if we get an error, we want to hold off on sending any more requests to that downstream system and then periodically ping it to see if this thing is still up. Another way we tried to handle that, to make sure we weren't overwhelming the API, was batching up our requests. So, instead of sending 100 requests with one return, we want to make sure that we send 100 returns in a single request. Batching those up is also one way to avoid overwhelming the API with tons and tons of requests. Ultimately, the changes that I made helped ensure that we were able to support all the returns. At the end of filing season, we were able to successfully file over 140,000 returns, and even across downstream outages or restarts, emergency deploys, we didn't lose any information.

The other thing that I implemented was that offline mode, that circuit breaker that I mentioned. So, the main idea there is that as we're processing, we might occasionally get errors. The API might say, "Hey, we're down. Hey, there was an issue processing things." We're holding off on submitting those returns because we want to make sure things are working. When they're finally ready to click submit, at that point, a request goes to the API, does some pre-processing, and then it puts a message on a message queue. We used to put it on an SQS queue, and then my application is reading messages off of the queue, doing a bit of processing based on some requirements, and then sending it directly to the API that does the actual processing. There are a few other downstream services as well, you know, to make sure it was rejected, to send emails out. There are a few other pieces, but my part was that second step. You hit submit, a message goes on the queue. My service reads that message from the queue, does some pre-processing, and submits it to that API.

One thing that caught my attention was your mention of the weekly meeting to collect and consolidate changes. Can you elaborate on how that process worked, and how you ensured that all stakeholders were on the same page?

In this case, we didn't have a hard SLA for my specific service, but as a product, we wanted to make sure that someone got a response within an hour. The reason I mentioned that is because the API itself was also asynchronous. When I submit it, it doesn't say this thing was accepted or rejected immediately. It gives me an acknowledgment. So, it says we've received it. We acknowledge that. And once we have that acknowledgment, we can later use that ID they give to say was this accepted, was this rejected. I don't know that they publish any specific numbers on how that API performs, but we make sure to store that information so that we can later check at some regular cadence, "Hey, have you processed this finally after you gave us this acknowledgment?"

I'm intrigued by your role as a release manager, and how you handled the challenges that came with it. Can you walk me through your thought process when making decisions about which changes to include in a release, and how you communicated with the team and stakeholders?

Certainly. So, one decision that we had to decide between, or one decision that I had to decide between, was where to store this intermediate information. One option was to just take advantage of S3 and just use that for keeping track of what returns still need to be processed, as well as storing that information temporarily. The other option was that I could do a split approach where I use a database instead to handle that batching. What I mean by that is one common pattern that you might see if you're processing things in batches with a relational database is I might have a database table that doesn't store all the information. It'll just store a URL that references where the information is, and you can essentially treat your database as a queue and have multiple different instances that kind of read, block a couple of rows, process them, and send it around. The other option was instead of using that database, to just stick with S3, and you can do some interesting things with the paths that something lives at in S3 to take advantage of to handle that batching. The ultimate decision that I went with was to use S3 because of time constraints. I didn't want to have to deal with introducing new database tables, introducing what could be a complex architectural decision, because beyond just writing the code, you have to maintain it. And if I'm the main one writing it, I don't want to introduce a system that would be difficult for other people to pick up. So, I didn't go with using the database as a queue approach because though it is nice, it can be fancy. I think it's something that if someone else has to come back and look at it, you could eventually figure it out, sure, but it's not a common way people might use a database. It's not a common way that people who aren't very deep into databases might understand how to use it. So, I went with S3 for simplicity's sake and for also making sure other people could easily pick up and modify the code as needed.

So that's one example where I had to work cross-functionally to understand what changes were going out, cut the releases, ensure we knew what was going out, and ultimately share with stakeholders how those changes might impact the service.

That's a great point about the importance of automation in the release process. Can you elaborate on the tools and scripts you used to automate the process, and how you measured the success of those efforts?

Sure. I think there are a few challenges, especially when you have a large engineering organization, because of how we chose to do releases as a release manager. One of the biggest things is there are a lot of changes, and as a developer, you might be heads down working on your change and might not know when it's going out. So one thing I had to really work on is overcommunicating. That means not only making a scheduled event and calendar saying, 'Hey, this is when changes are going out,' but also talking with team leads and making sure they know the schedule. One thing I had to grow into was instead of assuming everyone knew we'd get the code out on Tuesday, I made an effort to put it on calendars, make meetings, and reach out to team leads to let them know when things were coming up. I also had to be accommodating because there might be a late-breaking change; for example, we release our thing to a staging environment, and it turns out it doesn't work as expected. There's also that coordination of making sure those changes get in if you have that bug fixed before anyone sees it. The other side of that is also on the product side. I think sometimes there are gaps between what I understand as a developer and team lead and what product might understand as what features are going out. One thing I had to work with as well was that initially, I thought, 'Okay, I'll just generate the release log from all the commits that came out.' As a developer, I can read them. So I think communication was a really big part of what I had to grow into in that release manager role.
